question: Do the FAA and EASA specify the same maximum average burn length for self-extinguishing seat materials?

ground_truth.answer: No — they diverge: FAA AC 25-17 = 3 inches and CFR Part 25 = 8 inches, while EASA ETSO-C127c allows 6 inches.

question: Is the peak floor deceleration timing requirement identical between CFR Part 25 and EASA NPA 2013-20?

ground_truth.answer: No. CFR Part 25 requires peak floor deceleration within 0.09 seconds; EASA NPA 2013-20 requires it within 0.08 seconds.

question: Are the specimen conditioning relative-humidity requirements the same in CFR Part 25 and AC 25-17A?

ground_truth.answer: No. CFR Part 25 specifies 55% relative humidity; AC 25-17/AC 25-17A specify 50%.

question: How did AC 25-17A change the acceptable average burn rate compared with AC 25-17 (1991)?

ground_truth.answer: AC 25-17 (1991) required not exceeding 4 inches per minute; AC 25-17A raised it to 20 inches per minute.

question: Do AC 25-17 (1991) and AC 25-17A specify the same burner nozzle flow rate?

ground_truth.answer: No. AC 25-17 (1991): ~2.25 gallons/hour; AC 25-17A: 6.0 gal/hr.

question: Is the calorimeter placement distance the same in AC 25-17A and CFR Part 25?

ground_truth.answer: No. AC 25-17A: 8 inches (203 mm); CFR Part 25: 4 inches (102 mm).

question: Across the AC references, is the impact pulse produced as a deceleration or as an acceleration?

ground_truth.answer: Descriptions differ: AC 23.562-1 = deceleration; AC 25.562-1A = controlled acceleration (acceleration sled).

question: What distinguishes ETSO-C127b from ETSO-C127a?

ground_truth.answer: ETSO-C127b supersedes ETSO-C127a, carrying updated MPS/elective requirements.

question: Is the maximum average flame time after removal of the flame source the same in AC 25-17A and CFR Part 25?

ground_truth.answer: No. AC 25-17A: 15 seconds; CFR Part 25: 30 seconds.

question: For how long is the flame applied to the specimen — does AC 25-17 (1991) agree with AC 25-17A?

ground_truth.answer: No. AC 25-17 (1991): 15 seconds; AC 25-17A: 60 seconds.

question: Do AC 25.562-1A and AC 25.562-1B agree on what happens to the pelvic restraint during the acceleration pulse?

ground_truth.answer: No. AC 25.562-1B: the pelvic restraint must remain on the ATD pelvis; AC 25.562-1A: it becomes slack after rebound.

question: Is the date of the cited policy memorandum the same in AC 25.562-1B and AC 25-17A?

ground_truth.answer: No. AC 25.562-1B cites December 14, 2005; AC 25-17A cites July 7, 2003.

question: In which direction should passengers flow along the main aisle — does AC 25-17A agree with AC 25-17 (1991)?

ground_truth.answer: No. AC 25-17A: from the aft direction; AC 25-17 (1991): from the forward direction.

question: Is the minimum exposed specimen area the same in AC 25-17A and CFR Part 25?

ground_truth.answer: No. AC 25-17A: at least 8 inches by 8 inches; CFR Part 25: at least 2 inches wide by 12 inches long.

question: To start the burner test, does AC 25-17A swing the burner into position or away from it, compared with AC 25-17 (1991)?

ground_truth.answer: Opposite: AC 25-17A swings the burner INTO the test position to begin; AC 25-17 (1991) immediately swings it AWAY.

question: Do the FAA (CFR Part 25) and EASA (ETSO-C127c) agree on the maximum burn length, and by how much do they differ?

ground_truth.answer: They differ: CFR Part 25 allows 8 inches, ETSO-C127c allows 6 inches.

question: How does AC 25-17A modify the specimen conditioning temperature relative to AC 25-17 (1991)?

ground_truth.answer: AC 25-17 (1991) mentions 75 degrees F in one passage; AC 25-17A specifies 70 +/- 5 degrees F.

question: Compared with AC 25-17 (1991), what nozzle spray characteristics does AC 25-17A change?

ground_truth.answer: AC 25-17 (1991): 80-degree spray angle, ~2.25 gal/hr; AC 25-17A: 6.0 gal/hr nozzle delivery.

question: Is AC 21-25A still the applicable advisory circular today?

ground_truth.answer: No — superseded by AC 21-25B (lineage AC 21-25B -> AC 21-25A -> AC 21-25).

question: What is the current in-force revision of AC 25.785-1?

ground_truth.answer: AC 25.785-1B (it superseded AC 25.785-1A, which superseded AC 25.785-1).

question: Is the 1991 edition of AC 25-17 still valid for seat fire-protection compliance?

ground_truth.answer: No — cancelled and superseded by AC 25-17A.

question: How many successive revisions has AC 21-25 had, and which one governs?

ground_truth.answer: Three: AC 21-25 -> AC 21-25A -> AC 21-25B; AC 21-25B governs.

question: Has ETSO-C127a been replaced by a later revision?

ground_truth.answer: Yes — by ETSO-C127b.

question: Which document supersedes AC 20-146?

ground_truth.answer: AC 20-146A (AC 20-146 is cancelled).

question: Is AC 25.785-1A the current revision to cite for seat occupant protection?

ground_truth.answer: No — superseded by AC 25.785-1B.

question: Which advisory circular replaced AC 25.562-1?
If a document cites AC 21-25 (the original, no letter), is that the current guidance?

ground_truth.answer: No — it has been revised twice; AC 21-25B is current.

question: Between ETSO-C127a and ETSO-C127b, which is the superseding (newer) standard?

ground_truth.answer: ETSO-C127b supersedes ETSO-C127a.

question: Was AC 20-146 (no letter) cancelled, and if so by what?

ground_truth.answer: Yes — cancelled and replaced by AC 20-146A.

question: For AC 25-17 fire-protection guidance, which revision should currently be used?

ground_truth.answer: AC 25-17A (the 1991 edition is cancelled).

question: Is AC 25.785-1 (original) still the document to apply for seat installation requirements?

ground_truth.answer: No — superseded through AC 25.785-1A to the current AC 25.785-1B.

question: Which revision of AC 25.562-1 is the most recent in the corpus?

ground_truth.answer: AC 25.562-1B is the most recent (after AC 25.562-1A and AC 25.562-1).

question: Which FAA advisory circular governed seat fire-protection compliance in 1995, before AC 25-17A existed?

ground_truth.answer: AC 25-17 (1991 edition), effective from 1991; AC 25-17A only became effective in 2009.

question: Did Appendix J of the seat dynamic-test guidance exist in 2005?

ground_truth.answer: No — Appendix J did not exist prior to Amendment 25-72 (introduced with AC 25-17A, effective 2009).

question: Was Section 25.791 in force before Amendment 25-32?

ground_truth.answer: No — Section 25.791 did not exist prior to Amendment 25-32.

question: Which crashworthiness guidance applied to seats in the mid-1980s?

ground_truth.answer: AC 23.562-1, effective March 1983.

question: Were the EASA '9g seat' requirements of NPA 2013-20 already applicable in 2010?

ground_truth.answer: No — NPA 2013-20 dates from 2013.

question: Before Amendment 25-32 introduced § 25.791, was there a § 25.791 requirement to comply with?

ground_truth.answer: No — § 25.791 did not exist prior to Amendment 25-32.

question: In 2008, did Appendix J of the fire-test guidance already apply?

ground_truth.answer: No — Appendix J did not exist prior to Amendment 25-72 (effective 2009).

question: Which seat fire-protection burn-rate criterion was in force in 2000 — 4 in/min or 20 in/min?

ground_truth.answer: 4 inches per minute (AC 25-17, 1991 edition); the 20 in/min value came later with AC 25-17A (2009).

question: Was the EASA NPA 2013-20 vertical-velocity criterion applicable to a seat certified in 2009?

ground_truth.answer: No — NPA 2013-20 dates from 2013, after 2009.

question: Which document, effective 1983, addressed seat crashworthiness for small aircraft?

ground_truth.answer: AC 23.562-1 (effective March 1983).

question: Was the 6.0 gal/hr burner nozzle (AC 25-17A) the specification in effect in 1995?

ground_truth.answer: No — in 1995 AC 25-17 (1991) applied, with a ~2.25 gallons/hour nozzle; the 6.0 gal/hr value is from AC 25-17A (2009).

question: For a seat certified in 1992, which flame-application duration applied — 15s or 60s?

ground_truth.answer: 15 seconds (AC 25-17, 1991 edition); the 60-second value came with AC 25-17A (2009).

question: What are the specimen conditioning requirements (temperature and relative humidity) before the seat-cushion flammability test?

ground_truth.answer: 70 +/- 5 degrees F; relative humidity 50% (AC 25-17/25-17A) or 55% (CFR Part 25).

question: What are the two dynamic sled-test pulse levels required by the 25.562 seat tests (horizontal and vertical)?

ground_truth.answer: 16g horizontal and 14g vertical.

question: What injury-protection limits apply in the 25.562 dynamic tests (HIC and femur load)?

ground_truth.answer: HIC limit of 1,000 and femur axial compressive load not exceeding 2,250 lb.

question: What burner nozzle characteristics does AC 25-17 (1991) specify (spray angle and flow rate)?

ground_truth.answer: 80-degree spray angle, nominally rated at 2.25 gallons/hour at 100 psi.

question: What are the pass/fail flammability criteria for seat cushions under CFR Part 25 (burn length and flame time)?

ground_truth.answer: Average burn length may not exceed 8 inches and average flame time after removal not exceed 30 seconds.

question: Combining the EASA NPA 2013-20 requirements, what defines a '9g seat' dynamic vertical test (velocity change and pitch)?

ground_truth.answer: A downward vertical velocity change of at least 35 ft/s (10.7 m/s) with the longitudinal axis pitched down 30 degrees.

question: What conditions define the AC 25-17A oil-burner fire test setup (nozzle flow, calorimeter distance, flame time)?

ground_truth.answer: 6.0 gal/hr nozzle; calorimeter at 8 inches; flame applied 60 seconds.

question: What thickness must thick foam parts (seat cushions) be tested at, and under which appendix?

ground_truth.answer: 1/2-inch thickness, as required by Appendix F.

question: Why is the HIC limit of 2000 in AC 20-146A more permissive than the CFR value?

ground_truth.answer: False premise: the HIC limit is 1,000, not 2000.

question: Which section sets the maximum femur axial load of 3000 lb in the § 25.562 tests?

ground_truth.answer: False premise: the femur axial compressive load limit is 2,250 lb, not 3000 lb.

question: How does ETSO-C127c set the maximum average burn length at 3 inches?

ground_truth.answer: False premise: ETSO-C127c sets it at 6 inches; 3 inches is the FAA AC 25-17 value.

question: Which paragraph of AC 21-25C describes computer modeling for TSO seat changes?

ground_truth.answer: False premise: there is no AC 21-25C; the latest revision is AC 21-25B.

question: What is the 18g vertical sled test required by § 25.562?

ground_truth.answer: False premise: the § 25.562 vertical test is 14g, not 18g (the horizontal test is 16g).

question: How does NPA 2013-20 require a 55 ft/s downward vertical velocity change?

ground_truth.answer: False premise: NPA 2013-20 requires 35 ft/s (10.7 m/s), not 55 ft/s.

question: Which annex of AC 25.562-1C defines the 25g vertical sled test?

ground_truth.answer: False premise: there is no AC 25.562-1C (current is AC 25.562-1B), and the vertical test is 14g, not 25g.

question: Per AC 25-17A, why is the flame applied for 90 seconds during the oil-burner test?

ground_truth.answer: False premise: AC 25-17A applies the flame for 60 seconds, not 90.

question: Which transport-seat standard sets the Type I forward load at 12g under ETSO-C39b?

ground_truth.answer: False premise: ETSO-C39b Type I forward load is 9g, not 12g.

question: How does CFR Part 25 require peak floor deceleration within 0.05 seconds?

ground_truth.answer: False premise: CFR Part 25 requires it within 0.09 seconds (EASA NPA 2013-20 uses 0.08 s), not 0.05 s.

question: What is AC 25-17B, the latest revision of the seat fire-protection AC?

ground_truth.answer: False premise: there is no AC 25-17B; the current revision is AC 25-17A.

question: Why does AC 25-17A allow an average burn rate of 40 inches per minute?

ground_truth.answer: False premise: AC 25-17A allows up to 20 inches per minute, not 40.

question: Which section requires conditioning specimens at 90 percent relative humidity?

ground_truth.answer: False premise: conditioning humidity is 50% (AC) or 55% (CFR), not 90%.

question: How is AC 20-146B applied to seat certification by analysis?

ground_truth.answer: False premise: there is no AC 20-146B; the current revision is AC 20-146A.

question: What is the Head Injury Criterion (HIC) limit in the § 25.562 dynamic tests?
What forward load defines a Type I (Transport/Large Aeroplane) seat under ETSO-C39b?

ground_truth.answer: 9g forward.

question: What maximum femur axial compressive load is allowed in the § 25.562 tests?
What downward vertical velocity change does NPA 2013-20 require for the vertical test?
What is the horizontal dynamic sled-test pulse level for § 25.562 seats?
At what spray angle is the AC 25-17 (1991) burner nozzle rated?

ground_truth.answer: 80 degrees.

question: What flow rate does the AC 25-17A burner nozzle deliver?

ground_truth.answer: 6.0 gal/hr (0.378 L/min).

question: What thickness must thick foam seat-cushion specimens be tested at?

ground_truth.answer: 1/2-inch.

question: What is the maximum average flame time after flame removal under CFR Part 25?

ground_truth.answer: 30 seconds.

question: What is the vertical dynamic sled-test pulse level for § 25.562 seats?
Between FAA AC 25-17 and EASA ETSO-C127c, which allows a longer maximum burn length?

ground_truth.answer: EASA ETSO-C127c (6 inches) allows longer than FAA AC 25-17 (3 inches).

question: Which requires a longer flame application during the burner test, AC 25-17 (1991) or AC 25-17A?

ground_truth.answer: AC 25-17A (60 seconds) is longer than AC 25-17 (1991) (15 seconds).

question: Do AC 25-17A and CFR Part 25 specify the same maximum average flame time after flame removal?

ground_truth.answer: No. AC 25-17A: 15 seconds; CFR Part 25: 30 seconds.

question: A maintenance manual cites 'AC 25-17'. Should compliance rely on that 1991 edition or a newer revision?

ground_truth.answer: A newer revision — AC 25-17A (the 1991 edition is cancelled).

question: Is ETSO-C127a current, or has EASA issued a newer ETSO-C127 revision?

ground_truth.answer: Newer: ETSO-C127b supersedes ETSO-C127a.

question: Give the revision lineage of AC 25.785-1.
Which is newer, AC 21-25A or AC 21-25B?

ground_truth.answer: AC 21-25B is newer (it supersedes AC 21-25A).

question: Is AC 25-17 (1991) the active reference for the oil-burner test, or has it been revised?

ground_truth.answer: Revised — AC 25-17A is the active reference.

question: A seat certified in 1996 — which AC 25-17 edition applied to it?

ground_truth.answer: The AC 25-17 1991 edition (AC 25-17A only became effective in 2009).

question: Did the 20 inches/minute burn-rate criterion apply in 2007?

ground_truth.answer: No — in 2007 the AC 25-17 (1991) limit of 4 inches per minute applied; 20 in/min came with AC 25-17A (2009).

question: Was § 25.791 a requirement to satisfy in 1985?

ground_truth.answer: No — § 25.791 did not exist prior to Amendment 25-32.

question: Which crashworthiness AC predates the 2009 AC 25-17A by more than two decades?

ground_truth.answer: AC 23.562-1 (effective 1983).

question: In 1990, which crashworthiness advisory circular for small aircraft applied?

ground_truth.answer: AC 23.562-1 (effective March 1983).

question: List the AC 25-17 (1991) oil-burner test parameters: flame application time, nozzle spray angle, nozzle flow rate.

ground_truth.answer: Flame applied 15 seconds; 80-degree spray angle; ~2.25 gallons/hour.

question: A complete answer on the maximum seat-material burn length must cite which values across FAA and EASA?

ground_truth.answer: FAA AC 25-17 = 3 inches, CFR Part 25 = 8 inches, EASA ETSO-C127c = 6 inches.

question: Why does AC 25-17A apply the flame for only 10 seconds during the burner test?

ground_truth.answer: False premise: AC 25-17A applies the flame for 60 seconds, not 10.

question: Which paragraph sets the § 25.562 horizontal dynamic test at 20g?

ground_truth.answer: False premise: the § 25.562 horizontal test is 16g, not 20g.

question: How does ETSO-C39b set the Type I forward load at 16g?

ground_truth.answer: False premise: ETSO-C39b Type I forward load is 9g, not 16g.

question: Why is the calorimeter placed at 12 inches from the burner in AC 25-17A?

ground_truth.answer: False premise: AC 25-17A places the calorimeter at 8 inches (203 mm), not 12 inches.

question: Why does NPA 2013-20 require peak floor deceleration within 0.12 seconds?

ground_truth.answer: False premise: NPA 2013-20 requires it within 0.08 seconds, not 0.12.

question: What is AC 25.785-1C, the latest seat-installation advisory circular?

ground_truth.answer: False premise: there is no AC 25.785-1C; the current revision is AC 25.785-1B.

question: What is the maximum average burn length under EASA ETSO-C127c?

ground_truth.answer: 6 inches.

question: Under AC 25-17 (1991), how long is the flame applied during the burner test?

ground_truth.answer: 15 seconds.

question: What is the maximum average burn length under FAA AC 25-17 (1991)?

ground_truth.answer: 3 inches.
